name: Nausea
duration: Usually improves within 4-8 weeks as body adjusts
description: Nausea is the most commonly reported side effect of GLP-1 medications. It occurs because these medications slow gastric emptying and affect appetite centers in the brain.
management: Start at the lowest dose and titrate slowly
Eat smaller, more frequent meals
Avoid fatty, greasy, or spicy foods
Stay hydrated with small sips of water
Eat bland foods like crackers or toast when nauseous
Do not lie down immediately after eating
Talk to your provider about anti-nausea medication if severe
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if nausea is severe, persistent beyond 8 weeks, or prevents you from eating or drinking.

name: Vomiting
duration: Usually improves within first few weeks of each dose increase
description: Vomiting can occur especially during dose increases. It is related to the slowed gastric emptying effect of GLP-1 medications.
management: Eat slowly and stop eating when full
Avoid large meals
Stay upright after eating for at least 30 minutes
Try ginger tea or ginger supplements
Consider adjusting injection timing
Your provider may slow your dose titration schedule
whenToSeekHelp: Seek medical attention if you experience persistent vomiting, signs of dehydration, or vomiting blood.

name: Diarrhea
duration: Often resolves within the first month of treatment
description: Diarrhea occurs as GLP-1 medications affect gut motility and digestion. It is more common during the initial weeks and dose increases.
management: Stay well hydrated with water and electrolyte drinks
Avoid high-fat and high-fiber foods temporarily
Eat bland, easily digestible foods
Consider probiotics
Monitor for signs of dehydration
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if diarrhea is severe, bloody, lasts more than a few days, or you show signs of dehydration.

name: Constipation
duration: May persist throughout treatment but often manageable
description: Constipation is caused by slowed gastric emptying and reduced food intake. Eating less means less bulk moving through the digestive system.
management: Increase fiber intake gradually
Drink plenty of water throughout the day
Stay physically active
Consider a stool softener or fiber supplement
Eat fruits, vegetables, and whole grains
Establish a regular bathroom routine
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if constipation is severe, painful, lasts more than a week, or is accompanied by bloating and vomiting.

name: Abdominal Pain
duration: Usually temporary, improving as the body adjusts
description: Abdominal discomfort or pain can occur as GLP-1 medications change how your digestive system processes food. It is usually related to slowed gastric emptying.
management: Eat smaller, more frequent meals
Avoid foods that trigger discomfort
Apply a warm compress to the abdomen
Avoid lying down immediately after eating
Track which foods worsen symptoms
whenToSeekHelp: Seek immediate medical attention for severe abdominal pain, especially if accompanied by fever, as this could indicate pancreatitis.

name: Injection Site Reactions
duration: Usually resolves within hours to days
description: Redness, swelling, itching, or bruising at the injection site. This is a normal immune response to subcutaneous injection.
management: Rotate injection sites (abdomen, thigh, upper arm)
Allow the medication to reach room temperature before injecting
Apply ice before and after injection
Use proper injection technique
Do not inject into areas that are red, bruised, or tender
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if you develop a large area of redness, significant swelling, or signs of infection at the injection site.

name: Headache
duration: Usually resolves within the first few weeks
description: Headaches can occur as your body adjusts to the medication, changes in blood sugar, or from reduced food intake and dehydration.
management: Stay well hydrated
Maintain regular meals even if appetite is reduced
Get adequate sleep
Take over-the-counter pain relief as needed
Monitor blood sugar if diabetic
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if headaches are severe, persistent, or accompanied by vision changes.

name: Fatigue
duration: Often improves as the body adjusts over several weeks
description: Fatigue and tiredness can result from reduced caloric intake, changes in blood sugar levels, or the body adjusting to the medication.
management: Ensure adequate protein intake (0.8-1g per pound of body weight)
Maintain regular sleep schedule
Stay physically active with moderate exercise
Eat nutrient-dense foods rather than empty calories
Consider a multivitamin
Stay hydrated
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if fatigue is severe or interfering with daily activities.

name: Gastroparesis (Stomach Paralysis)
duration: May require medical intervention; usually resolves after stopping medication
description: In rare cases, the slowed gastric emptying caused by GLP-1 medications can become severe enough to constitute gastroparesis, where the stomach takes much longer than normal to empty its contents.
management: Report symptoms to your provider immediately
Your provider may reduce your dose or pause treatment
Eat very small, frequent meals
Avoid high-fat and high-fiber foods
Stay upright during and after meals
whenToSeekHelp: Seek medical attention if you experience severe nausea/vomiting that prevents eating, significant bloating, or stomach pain lasting more than a few days.

name: Pancreatitis
duration: Requires treatment; medication must be discontinued
description: Acute pancreatitis (inflammation of the pancreas) is a rare but serious potential side effect of GLP-1 medications. The exact mechanism is not fully understood.
management: Stop the medication immediately and contact your provider
This requires emergency medical treatment
Do not restart GLP-1 medication without medical clearance
Monitor for symptoms during treatment
whenToSeekHelp: Seek emergency medical care immediately if you experience severe abdominal pain that radiates to your back, especially with nausea, vomiting, and fever.

name: Gallbladder Problems
duration: May require medical or surgical treatment
description: Rapid weight loss from any cause, including GLP-1 medications, can increase the risk of gallstones and gallbladder inflammation (cholecystitis).
management: Report any right upper abdominal pain to your provider
Maintain adequate fat intake (do not go extremely low fat)
Lose weight gradually rather than rapidly
Stay hydrated
whenToSeekHelp: Contact your provider if you experience pain in the upper right abdomen, especially after eating, with nausea or fever.

name: Hair Loss (Telogen Effluvium)
duration: Usually temporary; hair regrowth occurs over 3-6 months
description: Hair loss associated with GLP-1 medications is typically caused by rapid weight loss and caloric restriction rather than the medication directly. This type of hair loss (telogen effluvium) is triggered by physiological stress on the body.
management: Ensure adequate protein intake (critical for hair health)
Take a biotin supplement
Maintain adequate caloric intake (not too aggressive a deficit)
Consider a multivitamin with iron, zinc, and vitamin D
Be patient - hair typically regrows once weight stabilizes
Consult a dermatologist if concerned
whenToSeekHelp: Consult your provider if hair loss is significant or does not improve after 6 months.

name: Muscle Loss (Lean Mass Reduction)
duration: Ongoing concern during weight loss phase
description: When losing weight with GLP-1 medications, 25-40% of weight lost may come from lean muscle mass rather than fat. This is a concern for maintaining metabolic health and physical function.
management: Resistance training 2-4 times per week is critical
Consume 1.0-1.2g protein per pound of ideal body weight daily
Spread protein intake across all meals
Consider creatine supplementation
Work with a personal trainer if possible
Do not reduce calories too aggressively
whenToSeekHelp: Discuss with your provider if you notice significant weakness or functional decline.
